_work.title: Wishlist Fills — High-Value Esoteric Cycles
_work.creator: various
_work.rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
_work.blurb: A curatorial gathering of specific high-value gaps: the Splendor Solis plate cycle, Sola-Busca trumps, Joachim of Fiore's prophetic figurae, the fifteen Behenian fixed-star seals after Agrippa, Horapollo's hieroglyphs, and further Aurora Consurgens folios.

entries.title: The Solar King and Lunar Queen Meet (Splendor Solis, plate 4)
entries.creator: Anonymous (school of Nuremberg)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: copy unidentified; cf. British Library, Harley MS 3469]
entries.summary:
A crowned solar king in red meets a silver lunar queen, the alchemical bride and bridegroom whose union begins the Great Work.

## Iconography
Two regal figures clasp hands within a richly painted Renaissance border of vines and grotesques. The king bears the red of sulphur and the sun; the queen the white of mercury and the moon. Their meeting stages the [[motif:coniunctio|coniunctio]] — the wedding of opposites.

## Significance
The *Splendor Solis* is the most lavishly illuminated of all alchemical treatises, ascribed to the legendary Salomon Trismosin and surviving in several painted copies from the 1530s onward. Its twenty-two plates narrate the opus as a sequence of allegorical tableaux. The royal marriage of [[motif:sol-and-luna|Sol and Luna]] is the first conjunction, dissolving the fixed and the volatile into a single body. Joscelyn Godwin and others read the cycle as a meditation manual as much as a recipe.

## For artists & game designers
The painted architectural border is itself a design system — treat each plate as a framed tarot-like card and the borders become a unifying UI motif across a deck.

entries.title: Knight Standing on Two Fountains (Splendor Solis, f.7, Harley 3469)
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.summary:
An armoured knight stands astride two fountains, one running red and one white, the guardian-figure who opens the *Splendor Solis* cycle in the British Library's dated 1582 copy.

## Iconography
The mailed figure plants a foot on each of two basins; a banner and shield bear solar devices. The twin fountains spill the red and white tinctures — sulphur and mercury, the two principles the adept must reconcile.

## Significance
Harley MS 3469, dated 1582, is the most reproduced witness of the *Splendor Solis*. The frontispiece knight is read as the alchemist-as-champion who must master the [[motif:red-and-white|red and white]] waters. The double fountain echoes the *Rosarium philosophorum*'s fons mercurialis.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made boss-guardian portrait: armoured sentinel over paired elemental springs, with heraldry you can re-skin per faction.

entries.title: Sun with Human Face Rising over the City (Splendor Solis, f.33v, Harley 3469)
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.summary:
A great golden sun with a human face climbs above a walled town — the rubedo, the final reddening that crowns the alchemical Work.

## Iconography
The radiant disc bears a serene human visage and rises over rooftops and river. The whole landscape glows with the achieved gold; this is the closing plate of the painted cycle.

## Significance
The anthropomorphic [[motif:rising-sun|rising sun]] signals the *rubedo*, the perfected red stone and the dawning of the philosophers' gold. In the *Splendor Solis* the sun-over-city closes a narrative that began with the king and queen meeting; the achieved Work shines on the ordinary world.

## For artists & game designers
Classic 'victory screen' iconography — a personified sun cresting a skyline reads instantly as triumph and completion.

entries.title: Splendor Solis — Detail of Plate 3
entries.creator: after Salomon Trismosin (attrib.)
entries.summary:
A cropped portion of the third plate of the *Splendor Solis*, attributed to the legendary Salomon Trismosin.

## Iconography
A fragment of one of the cycle's allegorical tableaux, isolating a figure and its painted setting from the larger composition.

## Significance
The attribution to Salomon Trismosin — supposed teacher of Paracelsus — is itself legendary; no such historical author can be securely identified. The *Splendor Solis* circulated as a prestige object among Northern European collectors of the sixteenth century. Detail crops like this one preserve passages of the painter's hand.

## For artists & game designers
Useful as a texture or inset: a single allegorical figure liftable from its plate for a card frame or codex page.

entries.title: The Philosopher with the Flask (Splendor Solis, plate 2)
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: copy unidentified; cf. British Library, Harley MS 3469]
entries.summary:
A robed philosopher holds aloft a sealed flask of golden liquid, the adept presenting the matter of the Stone at the threshold of the Work.

## Iconography
The sage stands within an ornamental border, lifting a round-bottomed flask whose contents gleam gold. A motto-scroll names the secret of the art.

## Significance
The flask-bearing philosopher is the cycle's emblem of the operator himself. In the *Splendor Solis* the held vessel announces the prima materia and the promise of the [[motif:opus|opus]]; the alchemist becomes both witness and vessel of transformation.

## For artists & game designers
The quintessential 'alchemist NPC' pose — robed figure presenting a glowing reagent flask. Reusable as a shopkeeper or quest-giver silhouette.

entries.title: The Drowning King (Splendor Solis, plate 7)
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: copy unidentified; cf. British Library, Harley MS 3469]
entries.summary:
A crowned king flounders in dark water, crying out as a figure reaches to haul him from the flood — the alchemical *solutio*, the dissolving of the old body.

## Iconography
The king's head and arm break the surface of a pool; a rescuer leans to grasp him. The painted border frames the drama of a sovereign nearly lost beneath the waters.

## Significance
The drowning king is one of alchemy's most arresting images of [[motif:dissolution|dissolution]]. The fixed, regal body must be undone in the philosophical water (*solutio*) before it can be reborn purified. C. G. Jung treated the motif at length in *Psychology and Alchemy* as a figure of the ego's necessary death.

## For artists & game designers
A potent peril-tableau: a king sinking in black water, half-rescued. Reads as a sacrifice or a near-death turning point.

entries.title: The Hermaphrodite with the Egg (Splendor Solis, plate 9)
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: copy unidentified; cf. British Library, Harley MS 3469]
entries.summary:
A two-headed androgyne — the *rebis* — holds a philosophical egg, the perfected union of male and female in a single body.

## Iconography
The standing figure bears two heads, male and female, on one trunk, cradling an egg. The painted border encloses this emblem of completed conjunction.

## Significance
The [[motif:rebis|rebis]] ("two-thing") is alchemy's signature image of the achieved [[motif:coniunctio|coniunctio]]: Sol and Luna fused into the philosophical hermaphrodite. The egg it holds is the sealed vessel of generation, the *ovum philosophicum* from which the Stone hatches.

## For artists & game designers
The double-headed androgyne is iconic shorthand for 'fusion' or 'final form' — a transformation reward or a boss in its perfected state.

entries.title: Venus and the Peacock's Tail (Splendor Solis, plate 16)
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: copy unidentified; cf. British Library, Harley MS 3469]
entries.summary:
Within a flask, a peacock spreads its iridescent tail — the *cauda pavonis*, the shimmering play of all colours that signals the turning of the Work.

## Iconography
A peacock displays its many-eyed tail inside a glass vessel set in a painted scene presided over by Venus. The rainbow of feathers stands for the rapid succession of colours seen in the alembic.

## Significance
The [[motif:cauda-pavonis|cauda pavonis]] is the iridescent stage between the blackening (*nigredo*) and whitening (*albedo*) — a sign that the matter is alive and changing. Associated here with Venus and copper, the peacock's tail is among the most beloved of all alchemical color-emblems.

## For artists & game designers
A flask blooming with a peacock's tail is perfect 'reaction succeeding' feedback — an iridescent particle burst inside a vessel.

citations.text: Lyndy Abraham, *A Dictionary of Alchemical Imagery* (1998).

entries.title: The Dark Sun (Splendor Solis, plate 19)
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: copy unidentified; cf. British Library, Harley MS 3469]
entries.summary:
A black sun broods over a dim marshy landscape — the *sol niger*, emblem of the blackening that opens the alchemical Work.

## Iconography
A darkened solar disc hangs above swampy ground within the painted border. Where other plates blaze gold, here the light is quenched: the sun of putrefaction.

## Significance
The [[motif:sol-niger|sol niger]] or black sun is the visual sign of the [[motif:nigredo|nigredo]], the death and rotting of the matter that must precede all rebirth. It became one of the most evocative images in the whole alchemical corpus, taken up by Jungian and later esoteric writers as a symbol of psychic descent.

## For artists & game designers
The black sun over a dead marsh is a complete environment mood-board: a cursed or corrupted-zone sky in a single image.

entries.title: The Sun Rising over the City (Splendor Solis, plate 22)
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: copy unidentified; cf. British Library, Harley MS 3469]
entries.summary:
The golden sun rises in glory over a town and its river — the closing plate of the *Splendor Solis*, the dawning of the perfected gold.

## Iconography
A great radiant sun ascends above rooftops, walls and water, flooding the whole scene with gold inside the painted border. This is the cycle's final tableau of achieved completion.

## Significance
The sun-over-city seals the narrative begun with king and queen: the [[motif:rubedo|rubedo]] has dawned and the philosophers' gold illumines the everyday world. Many copies place this as the twenty-second and last plate, a deliberate echo of cosmic renewal.

## For artists & game designers
A serene golden-hour finale — pair with the dark-sun plate (plate 19) as before/after bookends for a corruption-and-cleansing arc.

entries.title: Sola-Busca Tarot — Mato (the Fool, trump 0)
entries.creator: Anonymous engraver (workshop of Nicola di Maestro Antonio, attrib.)
entries.summary:
"Mato" — the Fool — opens the Sola-Busca trumps, the earliest surviving fully engraved tarot deck.

## Iconography
A standing figure in the antique manner is captioned MATO. As in the wider deck, the trump is named for a personage rather than the familiar allegory, in the all'antica taste of late-Quattrocento Ferrara or the Veneto.

## Significance
The Sola-Busca (c. 1491) is the only fifteenth-century tarot to survive complete and the first known to be printed from copperplate engravings. Its trumps abandon the standard Marseille images for a learned program of Roman heroes and obscure figures. The deck famously influenced the imagery of the Rider-Waite-Smith pip cards through Pamela Colman Smith.

## For artists & game designers
Named-hero trumps are a model for a 'legendary figures' card set — each card a portrait with a single caption rather than an abstract allegory.

entries.title: Sola-Busca Tarot — Trump 10
entries.creator: Anonymous engraver (Ferrara/Venice, attrib.)
entries.summary:
A numbered trump from the Sola-Busca deck, the earliest complete engraved tarot.

## Iconography
A single figure in classical dress occupies the card, captioned and numbered in the deck's antiquarian program. The engraving's fine hatching is characteristic of late-fifteenth-century North Italian printmaking.

## Significance
Each Sola-Busca trump replaces a conventional allegory with a named or numbered personage drawn from Roman history and legend, making the deck a humanist gallery as much as a game. It is the keystone object for the history of printed tarot.

## For artists & game designers
The austere single-figure-plus-number layout is a clean template for a minimalist trump series.

entries.title: Sola-Busca Tarot — Nerone (trump VIII)
entries.creator: Anonymous engraver (Ferrara/Venice, attrib.)
entries.summary:
The emperor Nero — "Nerone" — stands as one of the named trumps of the Sola-Busca tarot.

## Iconography
A figure in antique armour, captioned NERONE, embodies the notorious Roman emperor. The Sola-Busca peoples its trumps with such historical personages rather than the standard tarot allegories.

## Significance
Naming a trump for Nero reflects the deck's humanist conceit: the triumphal sequence becomes a parade of Roman exemplars, virtuous and vicious alike. Scholars connect the program to Ferrarese court culture and the antiquarian appetites of the 1490s.

## For artists & game designers
A tyrant-card: armoured Roman with a single ominous name. Drop into a 'historical villains' suite.

entries.title: Sola-Busca Tarot — Trump 07 (Deo Tauro)
entries.creator: Anonymous engraver (Ferrara/Venice, attrib.)
entries.summary:
Trump VII of the Sola-Busca deck, one of its named all'antica figures.

## Iconography
A standing classical personage fills the engraved card, captioned and numbered within the deck's learned scheme. The plate shows the deck's characteristic crisp linework.

## Significance
The Sola-Busca's numbered trumps form a continuous antiquarian narrative whose precise literary key is still debated. As the first complete engraved tarot, every surviving card is a primary witness to Renaissance print culture.

## For artists & game designers
Reuse the deck's principle: number each trump and let a captioned figure carry the meaning, no extra symbols required.

entries.title: Sola-Busca Tarot — Lentulo (trump VIII)
entries.creator: Anonymous engraver (Ferrara/Venice, attrib.)
entries.summary:
"Lentulo" — a member of the Roman gens Cornelia Lentula — appears as a named trump of the Sola-Busca tarot.

## Iconography
The captioned figure stands in antique military dress, one of the deck's parade of Roman worthies. Fine engraving renders armour and drapery in the North Italian manner.

## Significance
The choice of relatively obscure Roman names such as Lentulus underlines the deck's erudition: it presupposes a viewer fluent in classical history. This learned program sets the Sola-Busca apart from every other early tarot.

## For artists & game designers
Obscure-but-real historical names lend a deck depth and 'lore' without inventing anything; mine Roman prosopography for a card roster.

entries.title: Sola-Busca Tarot — Olivo (trump XVI)
entries.creator: Anonymous engraver (Ferrara/Venice, attrib.)
entries.summary:
"Olivo," trump XVI of the Sola-Busca deck, one of its named figures in antique guise.

## Iconography
A captioned classical personage stands within the engraved frame, numbered XVI in the deck's sequence. The hatched modelling is typical of the late-Quattrocento copperplate.

## Significance
Like its companions, this trump substitutes a named individual for the conventional tarot allegory, contributing to the deck's continuous humanist program. The Sola-Busca remains the foundational object for the history of printed and engraved tarot.

## For artists & game designers
The deck shows how a coherent visual style across many cards binds an otherwise idiosyncratic roster into a set.

entries.title: Sola-Busca Tarot — Ipeo (trump XVII)
entries.creator: Anonymous engraver (Ferrara/Venice, attrib.)
entries.summary:
"Ipeo," trump XVII of the Sola-Busca tarot, a named figure in the deck's antiquarian sequence.

## Iconography
The captioned figure stands in classical attire, numbered XVII. The card shares the refined engraving of the whole series.

## Significance
The enigmatic names — Ipeo among them — have prompted decades of scholarship attempting to reconstruct the deck's underlying literary or alchemical program. None has fully decoded it, which only deepens the Sola-Busca's mystique.

## For artists & game designers
Deliberately enigmatic captions can seed player theorising; an unexplained name invites lore-hunting.

entries.title: Sola-Busca Tarot — Catone (trump XIII)
entries.creator: Anonymous engraver (Ferrara/Venice, attrib.)
entries.summary:
"Catone" — Cato, the byword for Roman virtue — appears as trump XIII of the Sola-Busca deck.

## Iconography
The captioned figure stands in classical dress, numbered XIII where a standard tarot would place Death. The substitution of the upright statesman Cato is characteristic of the deck's moralised humanism.

## Significance
Replacing the Death trump with the exemplary Cato shows how thoroughly the Sola-Busca rethinks the tarot sequence as a gallery of Roman character. The deck thus reads as an ethical as well as a divinatory object.

## For artists & game designers
Swapping an expected slot (Death) for an unexpected exemplar (a virtuous statesman) is a neat way to subvert player expectations within a familiar structure.

entries.title: Sola-Busca Tarot — Bocho (trump)
entries.creator: Nicola di Maestro Antonio d'Ancona (attrib.), engraver
entries.summary:
"Bocho," a named trump of the Sola-Busca tarot, here in the finely preserved impression held by the Bibliothèque nationale de France.

## Iconography
The captioned figure stands in antique armour. This high-resolution BnF impression shows the engraver's hatching with exceptional clarity, the plate catalogued by Passavant.

## Significance
The deck has been attributed to Nicola di Maestro Antonio d'Ancona on stylistic grounds. Surviving impressions, dispersed between Milan, Paris and elsewhere, let scholars compare states of these earliest engraved tarot plates.

## For artists & game designers
A crisp reference plate — ideal for studying period engraving technique you might emulate in a stylised line-art card.

entries.title: Vaticinia of Joachim of Fiore — Prophetic Plate
entries.creator: Anonymous (after Joachim of Fiore tradition)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: digitised via Internet Archive Book Images]
entries.rights: Public domain (no restrictions). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A plate from the *Vaticinia siue Prophetiae abbatis Ioachimi* (1589), the printed corpus of pope-prophecies attached to the name of Joachim of Fiore.

## Iconography
An emblematic figure accompanies a cryptic prophetic motto, in the manner of the illustrated pope-prophecies. Beasts, popes and symbolic objects encode a forecast of ecclesiastical history.

## Significance
Though the Calabrian abbot [[figure:joachim-of-fiore|Joachim of Fiore]] (c. 1135–1202) did not author these *Vaticinia de summis pontificibus*, they circulated under his prestigious name through the late Middle Ages and were repeatedly printed. Marjorie Reeves's studies traced their immense influence on apocalyptic and reform movements.

## For artists & game designers
The pope-prophecy format — symbolic figure plus oracular caption — is a tidy template for procedurally hinting at a game's future events.

citations.text: Marjorie Reeves, *The Influence of Prophecy in the Later Middle Ages* (1969).

entries.title: Tetragrammaton–Trinity Diagram (12th century)
entries.creator: Anonymous (12th-century scribe)
entries.summary:
A twelfth-century diagram knotting the Trinity to the four-lettered divine Name, an emblem of the medieval theology of trinitarian *figurae*.

## Iconography
Interlaced circles or shields link the Persons of the Trinity, set against the Hebrew Tetragrammaton. The geometry visualises how three are one — the *scutum fidei* tradition of trinitarian diagrams.

## Significance
Diagrams of this kind belong to the same twelfth-century impulse as Joachim of Fiore's trinitarian *figurae*: the conviction that divine relations could be made visible through geometry. Such schemes fed both orthodox theology and later Christian-kabbalistic speculation on the power of the Name.

## For artists & game designers
Interlocking-rings 'three-in-one' diagrams make elegant sigils or faction emblems where unity-in-difference is the theme.

citations.text: Marcia Kupfer, *Art and Optics in the Hereford Map* (2016).

entries.title: Joachim of Fiore — The Seven-Headed Dragon (Liber Figurarum)
entries.creator: after Joachim of Fiore
entries.repository: Centro Internazionale di Studi Gioachimiti, San Giovanni in Fiore
entries.summary:
The great red dragon of the Apocalypse, its seven heads labelled with persecutors of the Church, from Joachim of Fiore's *Liber Figurarum*.

## Iconography
A serpentine dragon spreads seven crowned heads across the page, each head glossed with the name of a tyrant or Antichrist-figure; the seventh and the dragon's tail point to the final tribulation.

## Significance
The *Liber Figurarum* is the great picture-book of Joachite prophecy, in which the abbot's reading of history as three *status* — of Father, Son and Holy Spirit — is rendered as trees, [[motif:wheels|wheels]] and beasts. The seven-headed dragon maps Revelation 12 onto a timeline of the Church's enemies, a scheme that shaped centuries of apocalyptic expectation.

## For artists & game designers
A labelled seven-headed dragon is both bestiary art and a 'timeline of villains' UI in one — each head a chapter boss.

citations.text: Marjorie Reeves & Beatrice Hirsch-Reich, *The Figurae of Joachim of Fiore* (1972).

entries.title: Joachim of Fiore — Liber Figurarum, Tav. 11
entries.creator: after Joachim of Fiore
entries.summary:
Plate 11 of Joachim of Fiore's *Liber Figurarum*, one of the abbot's diagrammatic *figurae* of salvation history.

## Iconography
A geometric diagram — branching or circling forms — encodes a stage of Joachim's tripartite scheme of history. Labels distribute the persons and ages across the figure.

## Significance
The *figurae* are Joachim's distinctive contribution to medieval thought: theology made diagram. The progression of the three [[motif:three-status|status]] toward an age of the Spirit was read by Franciscan Spirituals and reformers as a prophecy of imminent renewal.

## For artists & game designers
Medieval salvation-history diagrams are a rich source of abstract, label-driven UI for tech-trees or progression maps.

citations.text: Marjorie Reeves & Beatrice Hirsch-Reich, *The Figurae of Joachim of Fiore* (1972).

entries.title: Joachim of Fiore — Liber Figurarum, Tav. 14
entries.creator: after Joachim of Fiore
entries.summary:
Plate 14 of the *Liber Figurarum*, a further diagram in Joachim of Fiore's visual theology of history.

## Iconography
A labelled figure — tree, wheel or interlaced form — sets out relations among the divine Persons and the ages of the world in Joachim's characteristic geometric idiom.

## Significance
Together the plates form a coherent picture-theology in which the structure of the Trinity is mirrored in the structure of time. This conviction made Joachim, in Reeves's phrase, the most influential prophet of the later Middle Ages.

## For artists & game designers
Pair several figurae as a 'codex' the player unlocks; their cumulative geometry tells a hidden history.

citations.text: Marjorie Reeves & Beatrice Hirsch-Reich, *The Figurae of Joachim of Fiore* (1972).

entries.title: Horapollo, Hieroglyphica (1595 edition)
entries.creator: after Horapollo
entries.summary:
A page of Horapollo's *Hieroglyphica* in its 1595 edition, the late-antique handbook that taught Renaissance Europe to read Egyptian signs as moralised emblems.

## Iconography
Woodcut symbols — animals and objects — accompany Greek-derived explanations of what each Egyptian sign 'means'. The format pairs a pictured emblem with an interpretive gloss.

## Significance
Attributed to a shadowy late-antique "Horapollo," the *Hieroglyphica* was rediscovered in 1419 and printed by Aldus in 1505. Its fanciful decodings — the scarab as the sun, the vulture as motherhood — convinced humanists that hieroglyphs were a wordless wisdom-script, directly inspiring the emblem books of Alciato and the speculations of Athanasius Kircher.

## For artists & game designers
A model for an in-world 'glyph dictionary' — each symbol an icon plus a one-line meaning the player decodes.

entries.title: Aurora Consurgens — The Building (Zurich, f.3r)
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.summary:
An architectural allegory from the celebrated Zurich *Aurora Consurgens*, whose lively watercolours illustrate the rising dawn of the alchemical Work.

## Iconography
A building or structure stands as an emblem within the manuscript's loose, vivid watercolour style — figures and edifices personifying stages of the opus rather than literal apparatus.

## Significance
The *Aurora Consurgens* ("Rising Dawn") is a fifteenth-century alchemical-mystical text once attributed to Thomas Aquinas. The Zurich copy (Zentralbibliothek, Ms. Rh. 172) carries an unusually expressive cycle of images that C. G. Jung and Marie-Louise von Franz made famous as evidence of alchemy's symbolic depth.

## For artists & game designers
The Zurich hand is wonderfully loose and folk-like — a reference for a deliberately naive, hand-painted illustration style.

entries.title: Aurora Consurgens — Sun and Moon (Zurich, f.10v)
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.summary:
Sol and Luna confront one another in the Zurich *Aurora Consurgens*, the cosmic pair whose marriage drives the alchemical opus.

## Iconography
A personified sun and moon face off across the page in the manuscript's bright watercolour, embodying the masculine and feminine principles, gold and silver, fixed and volatile.

## Significance
The pairing of [[motif:sol-and-luna|Sol and Luna]] is the central polarity of alchemy, and the *Aurora Consurgens* dwells on their union and strife in language borrowed from the Song of Songs. Von Franz read the cycle as a spontaneous symbolic process, a touchstone for the Jungian theory of alchemy as projected psychology.

## For artists & game designers
The sun-versus-moon face-off is timeless duality iconography — day/night, fire/water, two-player versus screens.

entries.title: Aurora Consurgens — The Zodiac (Zurich, f.11r)
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.summary:
A zodiacal wheel from the Zurich *Aurora Consurgens*, binding the alchemical opus to the turning of the heavens.

## Iconography
The twelve signs ring a circular diagram in the manuscript's watercolour hand, joining the labours of the Work to the calendar of the sky.

## Significance
Alchemy and astrology were inseparable: each operation had its planetary hour and zodiacal season, the metals governed by the planets. The *Aurora Consurgens* sets its mystical chemistry within this cosmic frame, a reminder that the laboratory mirrored the [[motif:cosmos|cosmos]].

## For artists & game designers
A hand-painted zodiac wheel makes an organic, lived-in calendar or time-of-day selector.

entries.title: Aurora Consurgens — The Peacock (Zurich, f.16v)
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.summary:
A peacock spreads its tail in the Zurich *Aurora Consurgens*, the *cauda pavonis* heralding the play of colours in the Work.

## Iconography
The many-eyed bird fills the margin in vivid watercolour, its tail a fan of iridescence — the visible sign of the matter passing through its rainbow of hues.

## Significance
The [[motif:cauda-pavonis|cauda pavonis]] marks the transition between blackening and whitening, when the alchemist sees fleeting colours bloom in the vessel. Its appearance in the *Aurora Consurgens* shows how thoroughly such workshop lore had become poetic image.

## For artists & game designers
A spread peacock tail is reusable as an 'unlocked the full palette' or rainbow-reward flourish.

citations.text: Lyndy Abraham, *A Dictionary of Alchemical Imagery* (1998).

entries.title: Aurora Consurgens — The Firebird (Zurich, f.23v)
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.summary:
A fiery bird — phoenix-like — rises in the Zurich *Aurora Consurgens*, emblem of the red rebirth that crowns the Work.

## Iconography
The bird flames against the page in bright watercolour, wings spread in ascent. Its fire marks the final reddening and resurrection of the matter.

## Significance
The [[motif:phoenix|phoenix]] is alchemy's supreme emblem of the [[motif:rubedo|rubedo]]: death by fire followed by glorious rebirth, the Stone perfected. The *Aurora Consurgens* enlists it among its menagerie of transformation-creatures.

## For artists & game designers
A phoenix in mid-ascent is the archetypal resurrection / respawn icon — fire plus upward motion reads instantly.

entries.title: Broadside on the Comet of 1618
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.summary:
A printed broadside depicting the great comet of 1618, sold to a public hungry to read the heavens for signs of war and woe.

## Iconography
A blazing comet streaks across a printed sky above a landscape or map, framed by explanatory text. Such single-sheet prints combined astronomical observation with portentous interpretation.

## Significance
The comets of 1618 coincided with the outbreak of the Thirty Years' War and unleashed a flood of cometary broadsides across Europe. Read as [[motif:celestial-portent|celestial portents]], they fed astrological prognostication even as Kepler and others began to study comets as natural bodies — a moment poised between omen and science.

## For artists & game designers
A comet-omen broadside is perfect environmental storytelling: a found document that foreshadows catastrophe.

citations.text: Sara Schechner, *Comets, Popular Culture, and the Birth of Modern Cosmology* (1997).

entries.title: The Lübeck Totentanz (Dance of Death)
entries.creator: Anton Wortmann, after Bernt Notke
entries.summary:
A long frieze in which capering skeletons lead away pope and emperor, merchant and child alike — the famed Lübeck *Totentanz*, the Dance of Death.

## Iconography
Death, figured as grinning skeletons, links hands with representatives of every social estate in an unbroken processional band. Each living figure is partnered by a corpse who draws them into the dance.

## Significance
The Lübeck *danse macabre*, originally painted by Bernt Notke in 1463 for the Marienkirche, was the most influential of all Death-dances; this image preserves Anton Wortmann's 1701 copy, the original having perished in the 1942 bombing. The [[motif:memento-mori|memento mori]] insists that Death levels every rank — a profoundly democratic terror.

## For artists & game designers
The estates-procession structure is a ready cast list: every social type paired with its mortality, ideal for a parade or roll-call of characters.

citations.text: James M. Clark, *The Dance of Death in the Middle Ages and the Renaissance* (1950).

entries.title: Behenian Fixed-Star Seal — Caput Algol
entries.creator: after Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa
entries.summary:
The talismanic seal of Algol, the "Demon Star," first of the fifteen Behenian fixed stars in Agrippa's astral magic.

## Iconography
A compact linear sigil renders the magical character assigned to Algol (Caput Algol, the Gorgon's head of Perseus). The seal is meant to be engraved at the star's planetary hour to draw down its virtue.

## Significance
The fifteen [[motif:behenian|Behenian]] stars — *stellae behenii*, from Arabic *bahman*, "root" — were singled out in medieval and Renaissance astral magic as especially powerful. Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa's *De occulta philosophia* (1533) gave each a seal, a stone and a plant for talisman-making. Algol, of baleful repute, was used for daring and protection but feared for its violence.

## For artists & game designers
These austere single-line star-seals make excellent rune or augment icons — pair each with a stone and a virtue for a crafting system.

citations.text: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia* (1533), Bk. I, ch. 47.

entries.title: Behenian Fixed-Star Seal — Pleiades
entries.creator: after Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa
entries.summary:
The talismanic seal of the Pleiades, second of Agrippa's fifteen Behenian fixed stars.

## Iconography
A linear sigil encodes the magical character of the Pleiades, the clustered "seven sisters" of Taurus. Engraved on its proper stone, the seal was held to draw the cluster's astral influence.

## Significance
In Agrippa's *De occulta philosophia* the Pleiades govern matters of vision, both inner and outer, and were paired with rock-crystal and fennel. As one of the [[motif:behenian|Behenian]] stars, the cluster carried a virtue thought potent enough to fix in a talisman at the right celestial moment.

## For artists & game designers
A seven-star cluster seal is a natural 'sight' or 'revelation' augment in a star-magic crafting tree.

citations.text: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia* (1533), Bk. I, ch. 47.

entries.title: Behenian Fixed-Star Seal — Aldebaran
entries.creator: after Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa
entries.summary:
The talismanic seal of Aldebaran, the red eye of the Bull, third of Agrippa's Behenian fixed stars.

## Iconography
A spare linear sigil gives the magical character of Aldebaran (Arabic *al-Dabarān*, "the follower"), the brilliant red star marking the eye of Taurus.

## Significance
One of the four "Royal Stars" of Persia and a [[motif:behenian|Behenian]] star in Agrippa's scheme, Aldebaran was associated with riches and honour, paired with ruby or garnet. Its seal belonged to a system in which the fixed stars, not only the planets, could be drawn upon for talismanic power.

## For artists & game designers
A red-star seal pairs naturally with fire or fortune augments — colour-code your star-magic tree by the star's hue.

citations.text: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia* (1533), Bk. I, ch. 47.

entries.title: Behenian Fixed-Star Seal — Capella (Hircus)
entries.creator: after Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa
entries.summary:
The talismanic seal of Capella — the "Little She-Goat," Latin *Hircus* — fourth of Agrippa's Behenian fixed stars.

## Iconography
A linear sigil renders the magical character of Capella, the bright star of Auriga that the older tradition names the Goat.

## Significance
Agrippa lists Capella (*Hircus*) among the fifteen [[motif:behenian|Behenian]] stars, assigning it sapphire and the herb horehound and connecting it to honour and esteem. The seals form a complete talismanic kit for star-magic in *De occulta philosophia*.

## For artists & game designers
The goat-star seal can anchor a 'nimbleness' or 'climbing' augment, tying mythic name to mechanical effect.

citations.text: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia* (1533), Bk. I, ch. 47.

entries.title: Behenian Fixed-Star Seal — Sirius (Canis Maior)
entries.creator: after Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa
entries.summary:
The talismanic seal of Sirius, the Dog Star (Canis Maior), fifth of Agrippa's Behenian fixed stars.

## Iconography
A compact sigil encodes the magical character of Sirius, brightest of all fixed stars, the heliacal rising of which once marked the flooding of the Nile.

## Significance
Agrippa pairs Sirius with beryl and savine, ascribing to it honour and the favour of the powerful. As a [[motif:behenian|Behenian]] star its seal channelled the most luminous point in the heavens — a star already sacred in Egyptian religion as Sothis.

## For artists & game designers
The brightest-star seal is a fitting 'legendary tier' augment — reserve it for the rarest crafting outcome.

citations.text: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia* (1533), Bk. I, ch. 47.

entries.title: Behenian Fixed-Star Seal — Procyon (Canis Minor)
entries.creator: after Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa
entries.summary:
The talismanic seal of Procyon, the Lesser Dog (Canis Minor), sixth of Agrippa's Behenian fixed stars.

## Iconography
A linear sigil gives the magical character of Procyon, whose name ("before the dog") marks its rising just ahead of Sirius.

## Significance
In Agrippa's *De occulta philosophia* Procyon is one of the fifteen [[motif:behenian|Behenian]] stars, linked to agate and the herb pennyroyal, and to the favour of gods and men. Its seal completes the pair of celestial hounds within the star-magic system.

## For artists & game designers
Procyon and Sirius make a natural 'lesser/greater' augment pair — same family, scaled effect.

citations.text: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia* (1533), Bk. I, ch. 47.

entries.title: Behenian Fixed-Star Seal — Regulus (Cor Leonis)
entries.creator: after Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa
entries.summary:
The talismanic seal of Regulus — the "Heart of the Lion," Cor Leonis — seventh of Agrippa's Behenian fixed stars.

## Iconography
A spare sigil renders the magical character of Regulus, the royal star at the heart of Leo.

## Significance
Another of the four Persian Royal Stars and a [[motif:behenian|Behenian]] star, Regulus signified kingship, courage and lordship; Agrippa pairs it with granite and celandine. Its seal was the talisman of rulers and the bold.

## For artists & game designers
The lion-heart star is the obvious 'valour' or 'leadership' augment — kingly gold styling, royal-star lore.

citations.text: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia* (1533), Bk. I, ch. 47.

entries.title: Behenian Fixed-Star Seal — Alkaid (Cauda Ursae)
entries.creator: after Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa
entries.summary:
The talismanic seal of Alkaid — the tail of the Great Bear, Cauda Ursae — eighth of Agrippa's Behenian fixed stars.

## Iconography
A linear sigil encodes the magical character of Alkaid (Benetnasch), the last star in the handle of the Plough.

## Significance
Agrippa places the tail of Ursa Major among the fifteen [[motif:behenian|Behenian]] stars, of a Saturn–Venus nature and a darker repute, paired with magnetite and chicory. Its seal belonged to the more dangerous reaches of star-magic, used in bindings and ruinous workings.

## For artists & game designers
A 'malefic' star-seal suits curse or debuff augments — flag its darker nature visually with cold or broken linework.

citations.text: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia* (1533), Bk. I, ch. 47.

entries.title: Behenian Fixed-Star Seal — Algorab (Ala Corvi)
entries.creator: after Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa
entries.summary:
The talismanic seal of Algorab — the wing of the Crow, Ala Corvi — ninth of Agrippa's Behenian fixed stars.

## Iconography
A spare sigil gives the magical character of Algorab, set in the constellation Corvus, the Raven or Crow.

## Significance
Agrippa ranks the wing of Corvus among the fifteen [[motif:behenian|Behenian]] stars, of a Mars–Saturn nature, joined to onyx and burdock and to workings of repulsion and ill will. Its crow-association links it to omen and necromantic lore.

## For artists & game designers
A crow-star seal is ideal for a 'malediction' or 'scrying' augment — the raven motif carries instant ominous flavour.

citations.text: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia* (1533), Bk. I, ch. 47.

entries.title: Behenian Fixed-Star Seal — Spica
entries.creator: after Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa
entries.summary:
The talismanic seal of Spica, the Ear of Wheat in the Virgin's hand, tenth of Agrippa's Behenian fixed stars.

## Iconography
A linear sigil renders the magical character of Spica, the bright star Alpha Virginis, imagined as a sheaf of grain.

## Significance
Agrippa counts Spica among the fifteen [[motif:behenian|Behenian]] stars, of a benign Venus–Mercury nature, paired with emerald and sage, and bringing favour, wealth and abundance. As the wheat-star it carried associations of fertility and harvest.

## For artists & game designers
The wheat-ear star is the natural 'abundance' or 'harvest' augment — green styling, fertility lore.

citations.text: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia* (1533), Bk. I, ch. 47.

entries.title: Behenian Fixed-Star Seal — Arcturus (Alchameth)
entries.creator: after Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa
entries.summary:
The talismanic seal of Arcturus — "Alchameth" in Agrippa's spelling — eleventh of the Behenian fixed stars.

## Iconography
A spare sigil gives the magical character of Arcturus, the brilliant orange star of Boötes, the herdsman who drives the Bear.

## Significance
Agrippa lists Arcturus (Alchameth) among the fifteen [[motif:behenian|Behenian]] stars, of a Mars–Jupiter nature, paired with jasper and plantain. Its seal drew on a star proverbially linked to storms and the turning of the seasons.

## For artists & game designers
Arcturus the herdsman-star suits a 'guardian' or 'driving the beast' augment — pair lore with a shepherding or taming mechanic.

citations.text: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia* (1533), Bk. I, ch. 47.

entries.title: Behenian Fixed-Star Seal — Alphecca (Elpheia)
entries.creator: after Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa
entries.summary:
The talismanic seal of Alphecca — "Elpheia," the jewel of the Northern Crown — twelfth of Agrippa's Behenian fixed stars.

## Iconography
A linear sigil encodes the magical character of Alphecca, the brightest star of Corona Borealis, the Crown.

## Significance
Agrippa numbers the gem of the Crown among the fifteen [[motif:behenian|Behenian]] stars, of a Venus–Mercury nature, paired with topaz and rosemary, and bringing honour and the love of others. Its crown-imagery made it apt for talismans of dignity.

## For artists & game designers
The crown-star seal is the natural 'honour' or 'charisma' augment — render with a small diadem motif.

citations.text: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia* (1533), Bk. I, ch. 47.

entries.title: Behenian Fixed-Star Seal — Vega (Vultur Cadens)
entries.creator: after Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa
entries.summary:
The talismanic seal of Vega — the "Falling Vulture," Vultur Cadens — one of the last of Agrippa's Behenian fixed stars.

## Iconography
A spare sigil renders the magical character of Vega, the blue-white star of Lyra, which the older astronomers pictured as a swooping vulture.

## Significance
Agrippa includes the Falling Vulture among the fifteen [[motif:behenian|Behenian]] stars, of a Venus–Mercury nature, paired with chrysolite and succory, and used for influence over beasts and for arts. Its seal channelled one of the brightest stars of the northern sky.

## For artists & game designers
The vulture/lyre star bridges 'beast-mastery' and 'art/music' augments — a fittingly dual-natured seal.

citations.text: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa, *De occulta philosophia* (1533), Bk. I, ch. 47.
